clean_story: Sun’s invisible breath.  Charged particles at 800 km/s. "Solar wind" isn’t wind—it’s the sun’s outer layer boiling off, a storm of protons and electrons ripped free by heat so intense even gravity can’t hold them. Imagine a pot of water boiling over—except the "steam" is a plasma hurricane, and the "stove" is a nuclear furnace the size of a million Earths. Every second, the sun sheds 1. 5 million tons of itself this way. Most blows past Earth, but some slams into our magnetic field like a firehose hitting a shield. When it does——the sky lights up. Auroras are just solar wind particles spiraling down magnetic field lines, colliding with oxygen (green) and nitrogen (purple), like neon signs powered by space weather. Strong gusts can fry satellites, scramble GPS, or induce currents in power grids. In 1989, a solar storm blacked out Quebec for 9 hours. NASA’s Parker Solar Probe now flies through the wind, touching plasma hotter than the sun’s surface. Three related subjects are space weather, magnetic reconnection, and coronal holes.
